front: A team member is underperforming. What is your first move?
back: Understand the cause and support them — coaching, training, or pairing with a mentor. Don't remove, replace, or report them first.
tip: Start supportive and private. Removal or HR are rarely the first step — escalate immediately only for serious ethics, safety, or policy violations.
source: PMP ECO — People domain; PMBOK Guide — Team performance

front: Coaching hasn't worked and there's no skills gap. What's the escalation path?
back:
Move up one rung at a time, documenting throughout:

- Agree a documented, time-bound improvement plan with measurable goals, coordinated with the resource/functional manager
- If it doesn't improve, escalate to the resource/functional manager and inform the sponsor
- Only as a last resort, involve HR to reassign or replace
tip: Personnel decisions usually sit with the resource manager, not the PM. Bring documentation and frame it around project impact.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='40' y='158' width='240' height='34' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='160' y='179' fill='currentColor' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>1 · Coach — understand the cause</text><rect x='40' y='112' width='240' height='34' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='160' y='133' fill='currentColor' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>2 · Document — improvement plan (PIP)</text><rect x='40' y='66' width='240' height='34' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='160' y='87' fill='currentColor' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>3 · Resource manager + sponsor</text><rect x='40' y='20' width='240' height='34' rx='5' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.6'/><text x='160' y='41' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>4 · HR — reassign / replace (last resort)</text><line x1='160' y1='157' x2='160' y2='148' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='156,150 164,150 160,143' fill='currentColor'/><line x1='160' y1='111' x2='160' y2='102' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='156,104 164,104 160,97' fill='currentColor'/><line x1='160' y1='65' x2='160' y2='56' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='156,58 164,58 160,51' fill='currentColor'/></svg>
figure.caption: Climb one rung at a time, documenting throughout. HR/replacement is the last resort.

source: PMP ECO — People domain; PMBOK Guide — Team performance

front: Two key stakeholders are in open conflict and it's stalling decisions. Best first move?
back: Facilitate a direct, private conversation between them focused on project goals and impact. Help them find common ground or a workable compromise. Escalate only if that genuinely fails.
tip: Collaborate / problem-solve is the strongest conflict mode; forcing and avoiding are usually wrong answers.

front: A new risk is identified during execution. Correct first action?
back: Record it in the risk register and analyse it (qualitative first), then plan an appropriate response. Don't jump straight to a fix or to escalation.
tip: Always go through the risk process. Jumping to action skips analysis and documentation.

front: A stakeholder requests a significant scope change on a predictive project. Proper first step?
back: Document the change request and perform integrated impact analysis (scope, schedule, cost, risk, resources). Then submit it to the Change Control Board. Never implement it silently.
tip: On predictive work, all changes go through formal integrated change control.
source: PMBOK Guide — Delivery & Measurement; integrated change control

front: The customer requests a change mid-sprint on an agile project. What do you do?
back: Welcome the change. Review it with the Product Owner and team, add it to the backlog, and prioritise it for a future sprint. Protect the current sprint goal.
tip: In agile, change is expected — the skill is staying responsive while protecting the current iteration.

front: You're unsure what's actually happening on the project. First move?
back: Assess before acting: gather the facts, review the plan and logs, understand the impact, then decide. Don't react blindly.
tip: On 'what should you do first?' questions, the answer is usually assess / review / clarify before acting.

front: The team is blocked by a significant impediment. Your primary responsibility?
back: Remove or mitigate the impediment and give the team the resources and support they need. That's servant leadership — not directing every detail or doing their work.
tip: Your job is to enable the team, not micromanage it.
source: PMBOK Guide — Team performance; Agile Practice Guide

front: Something serious has gone wrong. Should you escalate to the sponsor immediately?
back: Usually not immediately — first do what's within your authority: investigate, communicate with stakeholders, replan, and attempt resolution. But escalate promptly when the issue is genuinely beyond your authority or involves compliance, safety, legal, or ethical matters.
tip: Good PMs own problems and bring solutions or well-analysed options — not just problems.

front: You're tempted to add a nice-to-have the customer didn't request because it would be 'better'. Do you add it?
back: Don't gold-plate. Deliver only the agreed scope. Any new work goes through change control (predictive) or backlog prioritisation (agile).
tip: Trap: gold-plating wastes resources and creates uncontrolled scope creep.

front: An option says 'do nothing' or 'let the team handle it entirely'. Likely right?
back: Usually wrong. Stay engaged — facilitate, support, and keep ownership of the outcome. Empowering the team is not the same as disappearing.
tip: Eliminate do-nothing and extreme options early.

front: Best way to resolve a conflict or clear up confusion?
back: Prefer direct, interactive communication — face-to-face or a call — over email or a one-way message trail.
tip: Interactive beats push/pull for anything complex, sensitive, or emotional.

front: Two answers both seem reasonable. How do you choose?
back: After eliminating the extremes, pick the more proactive, collaborative, value-focused option that keeps you responsible for the outcome.
tip: When stuck, favour the answer that engages people, addresses the root cause, and fits the delivery approach.
source: PMP ECO — applies across all domains
